title: ombs_senegal
text: This file will become your README and also the index of your documentation.

title: ombs_senegal
text:
Developer Guide
If you are new to using nbdev here are some useful pointers to get you started.

Install ombs_senegal in Development mode
# make sure ombs_senegal package is installed in development mode
$ pip install -e .

# make changes under nbs/ directory
# ...

# compile to have changes apply to ombs_senegal
$ nbdev_prepare

title: ombs_senegal
text:
Usage

Installation
Install latest from the GitHub repository:
$ pip install git+https://github.com/iraind/ombs_senegal.git
or from conda
$ conda install -c iraind ombs_senegal
or from pypi
$ pip install ombs_senegal


Documentation
Documentation can be found hosted on this GitHub repository’s pages. Additionally you can find package manager specific guidelines on conda and pypi respectively.

title: Benchmark Models
text:
source

normalize

 normalize (df)



Split data


Feature generator

Feature generator that uses a sliding window of n previous timesteps and polynomial features to predict the next value, enabling learning of temporal patterns and non-linear relationships


source


FeatureGenerator

 FeatureGenerator (context_window:int=10, target_window:int=10,
                   degree:int=1)

Transforms time series data into feature matrices suitable for machine learning models. Creates lagged features using a sliding window and optionally generates polynomial features to capture non-linear relationships between variables.


Benchmark model
We chose a linear regression model as our benchmark because: 1. When combined with our feature generator, it provides a simple yet effective baseline 2. The polynomial features allow it to capture non-linear relationships in the data 3. The sliding window enables learning of temporal patterns across multiple timesteps 4. Linear regression models are highly interpretable and computationally efficient

source


SimpleRegressionModel

 SimpleRegressionModel ()

Initialize self. See help(type(self)) for accurate signature.


Model training and comparison
Now we create a single dataset containg all the results

plot_benchmark_scores

                        7), fontsize:int=7, xlim:tuple=(None, None),
                        ylim:tuple=(None, None))

Based on the scatter plot comparing MAE vs MSE metrics, we can conclude that polynomial regression with degree 2 and window sizes between 30-50 days provides the optimal predictions. This is evident from the cluster of points in the lower left corner of the plot, which indicates lower error rates for both metrics. Specifically, the combinations of degree=2 with windows around 40 days achieve the best balance between Mean Absolute Error and Mean Squared Error, suggesting these parameters offer the most accurate and stable predictions without overfitting the data.
We save the best model for later use
